item.title: Re: More from CtM?
item.content:encoded:
Short answer: my original idea for CtM, which I had since like 2013, was pretty much fulfilled by the Black Queen.

+ Long Answer
– hide block

My idea for CtM, or Cecile the Magnificent, was a French filmmaker who was absolutely obsessed with authenticity. To this end, he collaborated with alternate universe versions of himself to make anomalously-produced films.

His biggest problem: his work wasn't exactly… "high-brow". For all his experience with practical effects and the nitty-gritty of filming, most of his plots were at about the tier of Ed Wood. Blinded by the fact that it was all really happening, you know?, Cecile never really elevated any of his work beyond that, and pretty much condemned himself to obscurity.

Because directorship never made him too much money (which he never really cared for until it got in the way of either living or a project), he occasionally took lesser jobs, such as viral advertising campaigns (see 3428).

I don't really touch on him anymore since the Black Queen does what he does at a larger scope, but it was fun to think about.

item.title: More from CtM?
item.content:encoded: I came across this by way of 3248, which I landed on via 'random scp'. Really enjoyed both, and +1'd both happily! Are there more skips or tales that give more insight to CtM that I'm failing to find? If not, are there plans for it? :D

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Full licensing info:

Image is owned by FaceMePls, released under CC BY 2.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Image here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The image really sells this, the movie story makes this more interesting than the usual horrific reproduction cycle scips. I didn't mind the combination of effects, they do flow the theme of a trees reproduction, which is complicated. Ultimately it is another horrific reproduction cycle scip, so I stayed neutral.

However, pollinating insects really shouldn't have appeared spontaneously. It is so forced, and doesn't mesh with the nature of this thing. They could even ad something about a pest control program/protocol to make the containment procedure more interesting. "A bed? Pest control? What is this thing?" The reason for the bed was a pleasant twist for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So there are a number of things I'm not a big fan of in this scip. The use of the term "with extreme prejudice" sounds a bit off to me. Perhaps "are to be destroyed using all necessary force and resources" or maybe dedicating an MTF to the task. The use of "implement used for sleeping" could just as easily be "furniture used for sleeping" without the forced sense of SCIENCE! Finally I personally think there is just too much going on with this scip.
It's a nature god that infects hosts with a flower which then creates insects which then pollinate the god which then creates nectar which then gets imbibed (or fermented then imbibed) which then turns them into plant zombies which then attack potential hosts and infect them and turn them into plant zombies. That last sentence deliberately ran on to show how convoluted this scip is. It's a -1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The containment section about consequences for staff moonshiners is the essence of lolfoundation.
I generally like lolfoundation and I think this example is too much.

Any staff with access to the instructions would also have access to the reports on mutation. Absolutely no one would still chose to make the stuff for personal use.
Possibly to poison an enemy, but actual poison would be more effective, safer, and less likely to be traced back to the culprit.

Aside from that, the sequence of effects seem too unrelated. If it stopped at eye-flowers, it'd be nicely wtf and I'd upvote. If eye-flowers led directly to tree goblins, I'd probably upvote. But eye-flowers, to spontaneous bugs, to sap, to alchemical brewing, and then finally tree goblins is too adventure game moon logic for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So the item is a gift for making an movie, I wonder what happens if the movie was given an oscar?
…why would you try to make a beer out of it?

item.title: Junior Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

+ show block
– hide block

I wasn't quite sure what to do with this one until talking the author over IRC, and being told that the sender of the letter at the end is meant to be "CtM" from SCP-3428. A crosslink can be made to link the two together.

Changing this:

Best regards,
[ILLEGIBLE]

To:

Best regards,
CtM

If anyone has any other ideas, feel free to suggest them below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is 2 years old, but maybe the reason it's convoluted is because the movie might be a really silly B-movie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What? this isn't "Are We Cool, Yet?'s" doing? =/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kind of… kind of sounds like an AWCY? thing, not exactly but kind of. Upvoted with all my heart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too convoluted. It needs a sleeping person to grow a flower to make sap to distill to beer to make tree people. Too complicated. Cool image though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the note told the first person how to do it. so he would have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems a little haphazard to me, but i liked the note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to think the director was Ed Wood. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That really doesn't make sense, though. Sap contains sugar, so it can be fermented with yeast, so there wouldn't be any reason that anyone would try some ridiculously convoluted means of processing it, and thus nobody would ever have discovered its final anomalous properties. And if its being alcoholic is a side note, then it doesn't really bear mentioning at all, since most alcoholic drinks don't usually turn the imbiber into a plant monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The process to "refine" the sap is overly complicated. The alcoholic bit is just a side note if anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why on earth would the process of making the sap into alcohol be expunged? The process of fermentation has been understood for 5,000 years; add yeast, store in a sterilized container with an airlock, and let stand in a warm dark place for 1-2 weeks. Unless the process here is something other than using yeast to convert sugar into ethanol, there's absolutely no reason to classify basic chemistry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice picture, nice concept, nice job overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you gotta problem with footnotes punk

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ugh, footnotes are not your friend. Try to keep it under 3, or it's just ugly and annoying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how this is just one long string of random shit with various levels of "WTF is going on here?" It's great!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally got the picture working.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, it gets better towards the end, but overall it's a bit overly complicated. It grows roots that plant flowers that are used to make sap that can be made into beverage that, finally, makes tree people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found it pretty good. I'd remove the sentient bit from the first paragraph in the description, kinda feels like its giving away too much before the anomalous features are even described.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A bit of a jumble of ideas and concepts tentatively strewn together.

The B-movie bit is nice, but ultimately not enough to redeem it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You ironed out pretty much everything that I didn't like about the first draft, so no complaints from me. Awesome work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's close enough for government work. There are some tone and redundancy problems, but I am feeling of generous spirit, and I like the idea. My main objection to the draft (i.e. "how the hell would they figure out you had to ferment it??") is gone thanks to the addendum, so I'm firmly in the "I like it" camp.

+1. Welcome aboard, Skipper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I've got nothing more to complain about then. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Presumably there was, but it wasn't detailed in the report because it wasn't relevant to the object. There would be whole other documents about those investigations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not entirely sold on this, mostly because I kind of feel like there's a loose end concerning that movie. If there's an anomalous item like this that was used as a prop for a film, wouldn't an investigation be launched, or at least an agent sent out to make sure there aren't any 1285-3 instances that resulted from it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not particularly engaged with the idea, unfortunately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was sure that I would end up downvoting this. Then I got to the addenda.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was a little wary of the expungement and complexity of the thing, but the idea of someone using an ancient, magical/occult sculpture to make a B-movie was ultimately executed delightfully. Nice work.
